old_claim.note: Bronze Working provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct bronze-working dependency remains because the scoped ninth-century apparatus is documented with glass, pottery, wood, a stove, and charcoal, while the former generic materials citation supplied no bronze or copper-alloy apparatus evidence.

source_shape.source_locator: pp. 88-89 give the apparatus and procedure: cucurbit and alembic, glass receiver, earthenware or pottery bath pot, wooden support and cover, stove, and mild charcoal fire; no bronze component is specified.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: A complete implementation of the scoped medieval milestone is documented without bronze or copper-alloy manufacture.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The bronze_working node concerns copper-tin alloy craft, and neither the cited apparatus architecture nor the former NIST overview establishes that craft as a direct enabler of this ninth-century method.

ontology_before: Generic bronze-working capability was attached to distillation without source evidence for a bronze apparatus component.
ontology_after: The graph keeps only apparatus and practice prerequisites explicitly documented for the scoped medieval method.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-medieval-alembic-distillation-scope: bronze_working is absent as a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: A primary or authoritative source documents a bronze or copper-alloy alembic as part of the ninth-century milestone used for this node's date and scope.
The node is broadened to a later copper-still tradition where bronze-working capability is directly evidenced.
The bronze_working node is respecified to include the exact vessel-manufacturing practice used by the cited apparatus.
short_reason: The scoped apparatus is evidenced without bronze, and the old source did not license a bronze-working edge.
